title: Supply Chain Strategy
keyQuestion: How do we match our supply chain strategy to our products?
overview: Ever wondered why Walmart's supply chain is so different from Zara's? This lecture explains the Fisher Matrix - a simple framework that shows why functional products (like pasta or toilet paper) need efficient supply chains focused on low cost, while innovative products (like fashion or new electronics) need responsive supply chains that can react quickly to what's hot. We'll use Sport Obermeyer's ski jacket business to see how companies cope with uncertain demand through better forecasting, early sales data, and dual response strategies that combine both efficiency and flexibility.

coreContent.title: The Fisher Matrix: Matching Products with Supply Chains
coreContent.content:
Matching Products with Supply Chains
 | Functional / Replenishment Products | Innovative / Style  Products
Efficient Supply Chain | Match | Mismatch
Responsive Supply Chain | Mismatch | Match
Excess capacity & inventory
Long lead times
coreContent.keyInsight: Align your supply chain strategy with the type of products you are selling. Functional / Replenishment products => Efficient supply chain. Innovative / style goods => Responsive supply chain.

coreContent.title: Efficient vs. Responsive Supply Chains
coreContent.content:
Efficient Supply Chain: Match with Functional Products (Barilla Pasta, eBeer). Coordinate internally (S&OP) and with supply chain partners (CPFR and VMI).

Responsive Supply Chain: Match with Innovative Products (Sport Obermeyer). Use of information: Quantify risk (Newsvendor model), Reduce risk (Early order information). Use of flexibility / redundancy: Avoid risk (Reactive capacity), Hedge against residual risk (Common components and postponement).
coreContent.keyInsight: Different product/supply characteristics require different supply chain strategies

coreContent.title: Dual Response Strategy
coreContent.content:
Dual Response: Risk-based Deployment
Stable Factory. Tuned for stable production — ultra-low cost, but inflexible.
Variable Factory. Tuned for variable production — highly flexible, but at a premium price.
Source: Dr. Hau Lee, Stanford University
coreContent.keyInsight: Dual response supply chains can achieve both efficiency and responsiveness. Mixed strategies can provide both efficiency and flexibility (LL Bean)

coreContent.title: Sport Obermeyer Strategies
coreContent.content:
Obermeyer: Reduce Risk – Increase Return
Forecast distribution of demand
Assess overage and underage costs
Determine appropriate initial production quantities
Read early demand indicators
Update demand forecast
Determine final production quantities

Obermeyer Forecasting
Previous Method: Wally Obermeyer drives the point forecast
Current Method: Forecast independently → variance information
Average the individuals' forecasts to get the mean for the newsvendor model
Standard deviation of individual forecasts * 2 to get σ for the newsvendor model
coreContent.keyInsight: Improve forecast accuracy using early order information and independent forecasting to reduce demand uncertainty

coreContent.title: Coping with Supply Chain Risk
coreContent.content:
Coping with Supply Chain Risk
Use of information
Quantify risk
Forecast distributions, not just means
Make intelligent gambles with Newsvendor model
Reduce risk
Improve forecast accuracy – use early order information
Use of flexibility / redundancy
Avoid risk
Increase reactive capacity
Hedge against residual risk
Common components and pre-positioned inventories
coreContent.keyInsight: Use information to quantify and reduce risk, while building flexibility to avoid and hedge against residual risk

keyInsights: Three things that hurt you in operations: Setups, Bottlenecks, Uncertainty & Variability …but proper actions mitigate this pain
Align your supply chain strategy with the type of products you are selling
Functional / Replenishment products => Efficient supply chain
Innovative / style goods => Responsive supply chain
Dual response supply chains can achieve both efficiency and responsiveness
Different product/supply characteristics require different supply chain strategies
Use early order information to improve forecast accuracy
